claim: FDA issued a CRL for pimavanserin (Nuplazid) for dementia-related psychosis, citing lack of statistical significance in some dementia subgroups and insufficient numbers of patients with less common dementia subtypes as lack of substantial evidence.
reason: Lack of substantial evidence of effectiveness: insufficient statistical significance across dementia subgroups and too few patients with less common dementia subtypes.
precedent_implication: A single positive pivotal trial (HARMONY) can be deemed insufficient when the FDA expects evidence across heterogeneous CNS subpopulations; broad neuropsychiatric labels invite subgroup-level efficacy scrutiny.
source: Acadia Pharmaceuticals press release
quote: lack of statistical significance in some of the subgroups of dementia, and insufficient numbers of patients with certain less common dementia subtypes as lack of substantial evidence

claim: FDA issued a Refusal to File letter for NurOwn (autologous MSC-NTF cells) in ALS; the BLA did not meet the threshold of substantial evidence and the pivotal Phase 3 missed its primary endpoint.
reason: Refuse-to-file: BLA package did not meet the threshold of substantial evidence; pivotal Phase 3 had not met its primary endpoint (33% vs 28% responders on ALSFRS-R).
precedent_implication: FDA will refuse to even file a BLA for a CNS cell therapy when the pivotal trial missed its primary endpoint; patient-advocacy pressure does not lower the substantial-evidence bar at the filing stage.
source: BrainStorm Cell Therapeutics press release (PRNewswire)
quote: The FDA has indicated that the company can request a Type A meeting to discuss the content of the refusal to file letter.

claim: FDA approved zuranolone (Zurzuvae) for postpartum depression but issued a CRL for the broader major depressive disorder indication, finding the clinical evidence insufficient and requesting additional study.
reason: Application did not provide substantial evidence of effectiveness in MDD; FDA asked for one or more additional studies.
precedent_implication: A neurosteroid/CNS drug can win a narrow indication (PPD) while being rejected for the larger one (MDD) on the same molecule — indication breadth must be matched by indication-specific substantial evidence.
source: BioPharma Dive
quote: The FDA found the companies' clinical trial evidence insufficient proof that the medicine, known scientifically as zuranolone, is effective for MDD and asked for further testing.

claim: FDA issued a CRL for midomafetamine (MDMA) capsules for PTSD, determining it could not approve on the data submitted and requesting an additional Phase 3 trial; the CRL followed an AdComm that voted against efficacy and benefit-risk.
reason: Data submitted to date insufficient for approval; FDA requested an additional Phase 3 trial. Trial-conduct/safety-documentation and functional-unblinding concerns raised at AdComm.
precedent_implication: For a novel CNS/psychedelic modality where the drug effect is hard to separate from accompanying psychotherapy, FDA will require a fresh adequate-and-well-controlled Phase 3 regardless of effect size; an adverse AdComm vote presages the CRL.
source: Lykos Therapeutics press release (PRNewswire)
quote: The FDA request for another study is deeply disappointing...for the millions of Americans with PTSD...who have not seen any new treatment options in over two decades.

claim: FDA issued a CRL for AXS-07 (meloxicam/rizatriptan) for acute migraine for chemistry, manufacturing and controls (CMC) reasons only; the CRL raised no clinical efficacy or safety concerns and requested no new clinical trials.
reason: CMC: need for additional chemistry, manufacturing and controls data on the drug product and manufacturing process. No clinical efficacy/safety concerns.
precedent_implication: A CRL is not always about efficacy — a clean clinical package can still be rejected on CMC grounds; the flaw-surface a program must clear includes manufacturing readiness, not just trial outcomes.
source: Axsome Therapeutics press release (PRNewswire)
quote: The CRL did not identify or raise any concerns about the clinical efficacy or safety data in the NDA, and the FDA did not request any new clinical trials to support the approval of AXS-07.

claim: FDA requested voluntary withdrawal of the weight-loss drug Belviq/Belviq XR (lorcaserin) after a safety trial showed an increased occurrence of cancer.
reason: Randomized safety trial showed more patients on lorcaserin diagnosed with cancer (pancreatic, colorectal, lung); imbalance grew with longer treatment duration; FDA judged risks outweighed benefits.
precedent_implication: A CNS-active appetite/serotonergic agent can be pulled years post-approval when a mandated long-term safety trial surfaces a malignancy imbalance — long-duration carcinogenicity/oncology signals are a live withdrawal risk for chronic CNS therapeutics.
source: FDA Drug Safety Communication — FDA requests withdrawal of Belviq/Belviq XR (lorcaserin)
quote: FDA is taking this action because we believe that the risks of lorcaserin outweigh its benefits based on our completed review of results from a randomized clinical trial.

claim: FDA requested withdrawal of the Parkinson's disease dopamine-agonist pergolide (Permax) due to a high rate of heart-valve damage.
sponsor: Valeant Pharmaceuticals (generics by Par and Teva)
reason: Studies (incl. two in NEJM, Jan 2007) showed a several-fold increased risk of cardiac valvulopathy; a valvulopathy label warning (2003) and boxed warning (2006) had preceded the removal.
precedent_implication: An ergot-derived dopamine agonist's 5-HT2B-mediated valvulopathy drove a CNS drug off the market — receptor-level off-target (5-HT2B) cardiac-fibrosis liability is established FDA-recognized withdrawal precedent for dopaminergic/serotonergic agents.
source: NBC News reporting on FDA-requested withdrawal
quote: The Parkinson's disease drug pergolide was voluntarily withdrawn by its maker at the request of the FDA because of a high rate of heart-valve damage in users.

claim: FDA withdrew approval of Cylert (pemoline) ADHD products; the products had ceased marketing in 2005 because liver-toxicity risk outweighed benefits.
reason: Association with hepatotoxicity/liver failure (reports of hepatic failure leading to transplant or death well above the general population); FDA concluded overall liver-toxicity risk outweighed benefits.
precedent_implication: A CNS stimulant was removed once idiosyncratic hepatotoxicity accumulated post-approval — cumulative rare-but-severe hepatic failure signals are dispositive for a CNS drug whose benefit is non-life-saving.
source: Federal Register / govinfo.gov — Abbott Laboratories; Withdrawal of Approval of NDAs for Cylert (Pemoline) (FR-2023-05-23; products ceased marketing 2005)
quote: the overall liver toxicity risk of CYLERT (pemoline) (NDAs 016832 and 017703) and generic pemoline products outweighed the benefits of these products

claim: FDA withdrew valdecoxib (Bextra) from the market for cardiovascular risk and serious skin reactions (including Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
reason: Increased cardiovascular adverse events (notably in CABG post-surgical trials), lack of adequate long-term CV safety data, and an elevated reporting rate of serious/fatal skin reactions (Stevens-Johnson syndrome).
precedent_implication: A pain/analgesic COX-2 agent was pulled on combined CV plus serious-cutaneous risk with no demonstrated advantage over alternatives — for pain/CNS-adjacent programs, an unfavorable benefit-risk vs existing therapy plus a dermatologic SAE signal is withdrawal-grade precedent.
source: Wikipedia (valdecoxib), corroborated by FDA review memo (Apr 6 2005, fda.gov/media/74279) and EMEA suspension
quote: On April 7, 2005, Pfizer withdrew Bextra from the U.S. market on the recommendation of the FDA, citing increased risk of cardiovascular events and serious skin reactions.

claim: FDA directed a boxed warning on all antidepressants for increased risk of suicidality in children and adolescents.
indication: major depressive disorder and other pediatric psychiatric disorders
reason: Pooled analysis of 24 short-term placebo-controlled pediatric trials (>4400 patients, 9 antidepressants) showed suicidality ~4% on drug vs ~2% on placebo; no completed suicides occurred.
precedent_implication: A class-wide pediatric psychiatric efficacy program carries an FDA-established expectation of a suicidality boxed warning — any new CNS antidepressant must pre-empt the pooled-suicidality signal in its trial design and labeling.
source: FDA — Class Suicidality Labeling Language for Antidepressants (Drugs@FDA label, 2005; boxed-warning text)
quote: Antidepressants increased the risk of suicidal thinking and behavior (suicidality) in short-term studies in children and adolescents with Major Depressive Disorder (MDD) and other psychiatric disorders.

claim: FDA placed (2005), then extended class-wide (2008), a boxed warning on antipsychotics for increased mortality in elderly patients with dementia-related psychosis.
drug: antipsychotic drug class (atypical 2005; extended to conventional 2008)
indication: dementia-related psychosis in the elderly (off-label use)
reason: Placebo-controlled trial meta-analysis showed ~1.6-1.7-fold increased mortality (mostly cardiovascular events or infections such as pneumonia); 2007 epidemiologic studies prompted extension to conventional antipsychotics.
precedent_implication: Using a CNS antipsychotic in elderly dementia patients carries an FDA-mandated mortality boxed warning — a dementia-agitation/psychosis indication must directly address all-cause mortality, not just behavioral efficacy.
source: NIH PMC — Association of the FDA Antipsychotic Drug Boxed Warning With Medication Use and Health Outcomes in Elderly Patients With Dementia
quote: In 2008, the FDA extended the boxed warning to all antipsychotics based on reports of similar or higher mortality risk in elderly individuals with dementia taking conventional antipsychotics.

claim: FDA required lower recommended doses of zolpidem (Ambien and others) because of next-morning impairment, with a lower dose for women.
reason: Blood levels next morning can be high enough to impair driving; women clear zolpidem more slowly (peak concentration and AUC ~45% higher than men), so women's IR dose was lowered 10 mg to 5 mg (ER 12.5 mg to 6.25 mg).
precedent_implication: FDA will mandate sex-specific dosing for a CNS depressant when pharmacokinetics produce a next-morning functional-impairment (driving) hazard — PK sex differences and residual-sedation/driving endpoints are explicit FDA concerns for CNS sedatives.
source: PMC4560192 (analysis of the Jan 2013 FDA zolpidem dosing Drug Safety Communication)
quote: Women had peak serum concentration and area under the time-concentration curve that were 45% higher than men.

claim: FDA required class-wide boxed warnings on opioid analgesics and benzodiazepines for serious risks (including death) from combined use.
drug: opioid analgesics, opioid cough products, and benzodiazepines (class-wide, ~400 products)
reason: Review showed rising concomitant opioid+benzodiazepine use and increased overdose deaths; combined use risks excessive sleepiness, respiratory depression, coma, and death.
precedent_implication: Co-prescription of two CNS depressants (opioid + benzodiazepine) carries an FDA-mandated boxed warning on respiratory depression and death — any CNS depressant program must address additive CNS-depression/respiratory risk in combination use.
source: FDA — New Safety Measures Announced for Opioid Analgesics, Prescription Opioid Cough Products, and Benzodiazepines
quote: FDA is requiring boxed warnings — its strongest warning — about the serious risks, including extreme sleepiness, respiratory depression, coma, and death, from combined use of opioids and benzodiazepines.

claim: FDA approved removal of the boxed warning for serious neuropsychiatric events from Chantix (varenicline) labeling after the EAGLES trial.
reason: The EAGLES randomized trial (patients with and without psychiatric history) found the risk of serious neuropsychiatric events was lower than previously suspected; FDA advisory committees voted in Sept 2016 to recommend removal.
precedent_implication: A boxed warning is reversible — a sufficiently powered, FDA-co-designed safety trial in the at-risk population can retire a neuropsychiatric boxed warning, a precedent for de-risking a CNS-active drug's psychiatric-AE label.
source: Pfizer / Business Wire press release on FDA approval of boxed-warning removal
quote: The FDA approved removal of the Boxed Warning regarding serious neuropsychiatric events from the CHANTIX (varenicline) labeling, based on the EAGLES trial.

claim: FDA's PCNS Drugs Advisory Committee voted 0-10-1 against treating Study 302 as primary evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness, citing conflicting pivotal trials.
reason: On Nov 6 2020 the committee voted 0 yes / 10 no / 1 uncertain that it was reasonable to consider Study 302 as primary evidence of effectiveness; vote driven by conflicting results of Study 301 vs 302.
precedent_implication: An AdComm 'no' on conflicting pivotal trials is not dispositive — FDA can still grant accelerated approval on a biomarker (amyloid). But for an amyloid-targeting AD program, expect intense scrutiny of discordant pivotal trials and post-hoc subgroup reliance.
source: NIH PMC review — 'Aducanumab: evidence from clinical trial data and controversies'
quote: The committee members voted, with 10 members against and 1 member uncertain that it was not reasonable to consider the evidence of clinical benefit from Study 302 as primary evidence of effectiveness of aducanumab for the treatment of AD

claim: FDA's PCNS AdComm voted 9-0 that reduction in plasma neurofilament light (NfL) is reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit in SOD1-ALS, enabling tofersen's accelerated approval; a separate vote was 5-3 that clinical data did not provide convincing direct evidence.
reason: On Mar 22 2023 the committee voted 9-0 that NfL reduction is 'reasonably likely' to predict clinical benefit; a separate vote was 5-3 (1 abstention) that clinical data did NOT provide convincing direct evidence of effectiveness.
precedent_implication: Establishes NfL as an FDA-endorsed surrogate 'reasonably likely to predict benefit' for accelerated approval in genetic ALS — the first such CNS biomarker precedent. A program relying on NfL can cite this; one relying on direct functional benefit cannot lean on it.
source: Biogen accelerated-approval press release
quote: This indication is approved under accelerated approval based on reduction in plasma neurofilament light chain (NfL) observed in patients treated with QALSODY.

claim: The same FDA PCNS AdComm voted against AMX0035 in March 2022 (4-6), then reversed to support it in September 2022 (7-2) after new analyses of the existing CENTAUR trial — a rare same-committee reversal.
reason: Mar 30 2022: 4 yes / 6 no that the data established effectiveness. Sept 7 2022: reconvened, voted 7 yes / 2 no that evidence is sufficient to support approval, after Amylyx presented new analyses of the existing CENTAUR phase 2/3 trial. FDA approved Sept 2022 (later withdrawn 2024 after PHOENIX failed).
precedent_implication: FDA can reconvene the same AdComm and a reversal is possible on reanalysis of existing data — but the subsequent withdrawal shows approval on a single modest trial under patient pressure carries confirmatory risk. Single-pivotal-trial CNS programs should anticipate this scrutiny.
source: Amylyx press release (Sept 2022 AdComm supports approval)
quote: the PCNSDAC voted (7 yes votes and 2 no votes) that the available evidence of effectiveness is sufficient to support approval of AMX0035

claim: FDA's Psychopharmacologic Drugs AdComm voted against MDMA-assisted therapy for PTSD on both efficacy (2-9) and benefit-risk (1-10), citing functional unblinding and trial-conduct concerns.
reason: On June 4 2024 (NDA 215455) the committee voted 2 yes / 9 no on effectiveness, and 1 yes / 10 no that benefits outweigh risks. Concerns: functional unblinding, expectation/selection bias, abuse-related AE documentation gaps, therapy-vs-drug confound.
precedent_implication: Functional unblinding in a psychoactive CNS trial is a documented AdComm dealbreaker. Any program where patients can tell they got active drug (psychedelics, dissociatives, strong CNS effects) must pre-empt unblinding/expectation-bias critique or expect rejection.
source: FDA — Psychopharmacologic Drugs Advisory Committee, June 4 2024 meeting summary (midomafetamine/MDMA for PTSD)
quote: VOTE: Do the available data show that the drug is effective in patients with posttraumatic stress disorder? Vote Result: Yes: 2 No: 9 Abstain: 0

claim: FDA's joint Psychopharmacologic + Drug Safety AdComm voted 14-2-1 in favor of esketamine nasal spray's benefit-risk for treatment-resistant depression, supporting its approval as a novel-mechanism antidepressant.
reason: On Feb 12 2019 the joint committee voted 14 yes / 2 no / 1 abstain that the data supported a favorable benefit-risk profile. FDA approved esketamine March 5 2019 with a REMS.
precedent_implication: A favorable AdComm + REMS pathway precedent for a Schedule III CNS drug with abuse liability and acute dissociative effects — shows FDA will approve an abuse-liability CNS agent for TRD when benefit-risk and a risk-mitigation program are accepted.
source: Pharmacy Times / Janssen coverage of the Feb 12 2019 joint AdComm
quote: The committee voted 14 in favor of the data supporting a favorable benefit-risk profile of esketamine, with two voting no and one abstaining.

claim: FDA's PCNS AdComm voted unanimously that the Clarity AD trial confirmed the clinical benefit of lecanemab, supporting traditional approval of an anti-amyloid AD drug — the positive contrast to aducanumab.
reason: On June 9 2023 the committee voted unanimously that the Phase 3 Clarity AD data confirm clinical benefit and a favorable benefit-risk profile. FDA granted traditional approval July 6 2023.
precedent_implication: A single positive, adequately-powered confirmatory trial (Clarity AD) flipped the amyloid hypothesis from the aducanumab debacle to a clean unanimous endorsement. The discriminator for amyloid-targeting AD programs is a convincing, non-discordant pivotal trial — not the target class itself.
source: BioArctic / Biogen press release on the June 9 2023 AdComm
quote: The committee voted unanimously that the data confirms the clinical benefit of LEQEMBI for the treatment of early Alzheimer's disease.

claim: FDA granted accelerated approval to Aduhelm (aducanumab) for Alzheimer's based on reduction of amyloid-beta plaque as a surrogate endpoint, overriding the Nov-2020 AdComm vote; later discontinued by Biogen.
reason: Accelerated approval on amyloid-beta-plaque surrogate (not established clinical benefit); reversed a Nov-2020 AdComm no vote; later discontinued by Biogen. Multiple committee members resigned in protest.
precedent_implication: AdComm outcomes are advisory — a negative vote is not a dispositive veto, and the dispositive action is the FDA's own decision. FDA will accept an amyloid-burden surrogate for accelerated approval in AD, but the controversy/discontinuation makes amyloid-surrogate-only AD programs a high-scrutiny precedent.
source: Alzforum — Aduhelm (aducanumab) therapeutics record (FDA accelerated-approval action)
quote: On June 7, the FDA approved aducanumab under the agency's accelerated approval pathway, which requires substantial evidence of effect on an intermediate marker (amyloid removal), reasonable likelihood of a meaningful clinical benefit, and Phase 4 evidence for such a benefit to be gathered in a subsequent trial, after the marketing license has been granted

claim: FDA converted Leqembi (lecanemab) to traditional approval for early Alzheimer's after the CLARITY AD confirmatory trial showed statistically significant slowing of decline; it had been accelerated-approved in Jan 2023 on the amyloid-plaque surrogate.
reason: Traditional approval after confirmatory clinical benefit (CLARITY AD); prior accelerated approval Jan 2023 on amyloid surrogate.
precedent_implication: The successful surrogate-to-clinical conversion that aducanumab never achieved — the template FDA now expects an amyloid program to follow: surrogate gets you in, a positive clinical confirmatory trial earns full approval.
source: Eisai (FDA traditional approval)
quote: On July 6, 2023, FDA approved the sBLA supporting traditional approval of LEQEMBI, the first treatment shown to reduce the rate of disease progression in Alzheimer's disease.

claim: FDA granted accelerated approval to Qalsody (tofersen), an intrathecal antisense oligonucleotide, for SOD1-ALS based on reduction in plasma neurofilament light (NfL); continued approval contingent on the ATLAS confirmatory trial.
reason: Accelerated approval on a neurofilament (NfL) biomarker surrogate after the VALOR primary clinical endpoint was not met; ATLAS is the confirmatory study.
precedent_implication: FDA will accept plasma NfL as a surrogate for accelerated approval in a genetically defined CNS/neurodegenerative population even when the primary clinical endpoint missed — a key precedent for biomarker-led ALS/neurodegeneration programs.
source: Oligonucleotide Therapeutics Society / FDA NDA 215887
quote: This indication is approved under accelerated approval based on reduction in plasma neurofilament light chain (NfL) observed in patients treated with QALSODY.

claim: FDA granted accelerated approval to Exondys 51 (eteplirsen), an exon-51-skipping antisense oligonucleotide, for Duchenne muscular dystrophy based on the surrogate endpoint of increased dystrophin in skeletal muscle; clinical benefit was not established at approval.
reason: Accelerated approval on a dystrophin-production surrogate reasonably likely to predict benefit; clinical benefit (motor function) not established, confirmatory trial required.
precedent_implication: Landmark precedent that FDA will accept a small, contested protein-biomarker surrogate (dystrophin) over advisory-committee skepticism in a fatal rare neuromuscular disease — frequently cited both as a pathway opener and as a cautionary low-evidence approval.
source: NIH PMC — 'Eteplirsen: First Global Approval' (reproduces the FDA accelerated-approval action) / FDA NDA 206488
quote: Eteplirsen was granted accelerated approval on the basis of surrogate end-point results showing that it was able to increase dystrophin levels in patients.

claim: FDA approved Spinraza (nusinersen), an intrathecal antisense oligonucleotide, for spinal muscular atrophy via standard approval on a clinical endpoint (motor-milestone response in ENDEAR, 40% vs 0%), not a surrogate-only accelerated approval.
reason: Standard approval on a demonstrated clinical (motor-milestone) benefit plus survival in genetically defined SMA.
precedent_implication: The contrast case: a genetically defined CNS disease approved on a real clinical endpoint, showing FDA prefers a clinical readout where achievable — sets the bar surrogate-based CNS programs are measured against.
source: Biogen/Ionis via PR Newswire (FDA approval)
quote: A greater percentage of infants treated with SPINRAZA achieved a motor milestone response compared to those who did not receive treatment (40% versus 0%; p<0.0001).

claim: FDA placed a clinical hold on Pfizer's mini-dystrophin gene therapy (fordadistrogene movaparvovec, PF-06939926) for Duchenne muscular dystrophy after a patient died in a Phase 1b study; the hold was later lifted.
reason: Patient death in a Phase 1b non-ambulatory cohort; FDA clinical hold on the IND (preceded by SAEs of muscle weakness/myocarditis).
precedent_implication: Precedent that a single trial death triggers an FDA clinical hold on a CNS/neuromuscular gene therapy IND — programs with AAV-delivery cardiac/muscle safety signals should expect hold risk.
source: pharmaphorum
quote: The pause – which has been accompanied by a clinical hold imposed by the FDA – applies to a phase 1b trial.

claim: FDA placed clinical holds on Sarepta's gene therapy trials (SRP-9004 and SRP-9003), requested suspension of Elevidys (delandistrogene moxeparvovec) distribution, and revoked the AAVrh74 platform technology designation after three patient deaths from acute liver failure.
reason: Three deaths from acute liver failure across AAVrh74-platform products; FDA clinical holds, requested market suspension of the approved Elevidys, and platform-designation revocation.
precedent_implication: Strong recent precedent: FDA will hold an entire AAV gene-therapy platform and move against an already-approved CNS/neuromuscular product on a shared mechanistic liability (hepatotoxicity) — a class/platform safety signal can gate every program built on it.
source: CGTLive (carrying FDA action)
quote: The FDA placed clinical holds on trials for SRP-9004 and SRP-9003, requested suspension of Elevidys distribution, and revoked the AAVrh74 platform technology designation following three patient deaths attributed to acute liver failure.

claim: FDA's Early Alzheimer's Disease guidance allows biomarker improvement alone to support success in presymptomatic Stage 1 and neuropsychological-test improvement in Stage 2, reserving functional co-primary endpoints for symptomatic stages.
reason: Stage-dependent endpoint framework: in presymptomatic stages a biomarker or cognitive-test effect can indicate treatment success; functional improvement remains the threshold only in symptomatic stages.
precedent_implication: An early-AD program staged as preclinical can pursue a biomarker/cognitive endpoint, but a symptomatic-stage indication must still show a clinically meaningful functional effect.
source: FDA (verified via NIH PMC6804505)
quote: In these presymptomatic stages, a path forward can be found for drug approval with biomarker improvement sufficient to indicate a treatment success in stage 1.

claim: FDA's final ALS drug-development guidance (Sept 2019) advises trial designs that minimize placebo-only exposure and addresses muscle weakness and survival as the disease's core motor aspects.
reason: Guidance on clinical development/trial design for ALS; emphasizes earlier sponsor communication, broader inclusion criteria, and avoiding denying patients effective therapy via placebo-only randomization.
precedent_implication: An ALS program is expected to target the main motor aspects (weakness/survival) and to justify any placebo-only design against FDA's stated preference to limit it.
source: FDA / Federal Register 2019-20629
quote: The guidance provides FDA's current thinking on the clinical development program and clinical trial designs for drugs to support an indication for the treatment of amyotrophic lateral sclerosis.

claim: FDA's 2018 draft MDD guidance revised the 1977 antidepressant guidance and addresses development programs and trial designs for monotherapy, combination, and adjunctive MDD treatments.
reason: First major update since the 1977 'Guidelines for the Clinical Evaluation of Antidepressant Drugs'; covers trial design for monotherapeutic, combination, and adjunctive antidepressant products.
precedent_implication: An MDD program's trial design (including adjunctive/combination claims and relapse-prevention) is judged against this modernized framework, not the legacy 1977 expectations.
source: FDA / Federal Register 2018-13297
quote: This draft guidance revises the guidance for industry entitled 'Guidelines for the Clinical Evaluation of Antidepressant Drugs' issued in September 1977.

claim: FDA concluded that efficacy of drugs approved for partial-onset seizures in adults can be fully extrapolated to pediatric patients 1 month of age and older, requiring PK and long-term open-label safety data rather than independent pediatric efficacy trials.
reason: Disease similarity, similar response to intervention, and similar exposure-response between adults and children justify efficacy extrapolation for POS; PK matching to adult exposures plus long-term pediatric safety data required.
precedent_implication: A POS antiseizure program can avoid a dedicated pediatric efficacy trial down to 1 month of age, but cannot extrapolate to other seizure types and still owes PK/safety bridging data.
source: FDA final guidance — Drugs for the Treatment of Partial Onset Seizures: Extrapolation of Efficacy from Adults to Pediatric Patients 1 Month of Age and Older
quote: Specifically, this guidance addresses FDA's current thinking regarding clinical development programs that can support extrapolation of the efficacy of drugs approved for the treatment of POS in adults to pediatric patients 1 month of age and older.

claim: FDA's migraine acute-treatment guidance (Oct 2014) replaced the previous four co-primary endpoints with two co-primaries: pain freedom and freedom from the most bothersome symptom, both at 2 hours post-treatment.
reason: Endpoint simplification from four co-primaries (pain, nausea, photophobia, phonophobia freedom) to two co-primaries (pain freedom + most-bothersome-symptom freedom) at 2 hours.
precedent_implication: An acute-migraine program is expected to power on the two-co-primary (pain freedom + MBS) design at 2 hours; the older four-endpoint design is no longer the standard.
source: FDA (verified via PubMed 30953576)
quote: This guidance offered the option of replacing the previously required 4 co-primary endpoints...with 2 co-primary endpoints: pain freedom and freedom from most bothersome symptom (MBS) other than pain, both at 2 hours posttreatment.

claim: FDA's non-opioid analgesic guidances prefer patient-reported pain intensity as the primary endpoint and caution that accelerated approval is hard to use because no surrogate/intermediate endpoint is established to predict an analgesic effect.
reason: Patient-reported pain intensity is the preferred primary endpoint with function/QoL secondary; pain's subjective nature makes a surrogate-endpoint-based accelerated approval pathway difficult.
precedent_implication: A CNS analgesic program should anchor on a validated patient-reported pain-intensity primary endpoint and should not assume an accelerated-approval surrogate pathway.
source: FDA (Development of Non-Opioid Analgesics for Acute Pain draft guidance)
quote: It is difficult to envision how surrogate or intermediate endpoints could be used to predict analgesic effect.

claim: FDA's DMD guidance (Feb 2018) declines to prescribe required clinical outcomes, asks sponsors to validate endpoints across disease stages, and accepts dystrophin/muscle biomarkers as surrogate endpoints supporting accelerated approval in some situations.
reason: No defined required clinical outcomes; biomarkers reflecting skeletal-muscle health/amount may serve as surrogate endpoints for accelerated approval; ~1 year safety exposure generally appropriate.
precedent_implication: A DMD program may pursue accelerated approval on a muscle/dystrophin biomarker surrogate, but must propose/validate clinical endpoints and supply ~1 year of safety exposure for full approval.
source: FDA / Federal Register 2018-03225
quote: FDA recommends use of biomarkers that reliably reflect the health and amount of skeletal muscle...as surrogate endpoints to support accelerated approval.

claim: Under FDA's accelerated-approval framework, a 'reasonably likely surrogate endpoint' — supported by mechanistic/epidemiologic rationale but not yet validated — may support approval, conditioned on a required post-approval confirmatory trial.
indication: Serious diseases (cross-cutting; applies to CNS surrogate endpoints)
reason: A reasonably likely surrogate endpoint has strong mechanistic/epidemiologic support but insufficient clinical data to be validated; it can support accelerated approval with a mandatory confirmatory trial.
precedent_implication: A CNS program seeking accelerated approval on a biomarker must argue the endpoint is 'reasonably likely to predict clinical benefit' and commit to a confirmatory trial, or risk withdrawal.
source: FDA BEST Resource (NCBI NBK453485)
quote: Such endpoints may be used for accelerated approval for drugs and potentially also for approval or clearance of medical devices.
